04 The Scalable Planning System - YouTube.mp4
Literally every book I've ever read on the subject of strategic planning and goal setting follows the same general cadence. Goes something like this. You set a five or even ten-year vision. You build an annual plan, usually in December, and you review and adjust that plan monthly. Does this sound familiar? Now, on the surface, this... planning framework sounds good, which is probably why everyone teaches it this way. But in 20 plus years of starting and running entrepreneurial companies, I don't know anyone— who has actually executed this framework with any degree of accuracy over time. Now, I freely admit that this model is better than nothing, but there's still three big problems with this supposedly tried-and-true strategic planning framework. First, ten and even five-year goals are ludicrously long. For most entrepreneurial companies.
Now, don't get me wrong, I love a big, hairy, audacious goal as much as the next guy, but can we seriously think that we know where... we're going to want to be in 10 years? I mean, I can't take those kinds of timelines seriously. So how can I expect my team to take them seriously? Second, as we've already discussed. Annual plans are a joke. They are too long to be predictable and too short to be meaningful. Which is why most annual plans, just like most New Year's resolutions, are typically abandoned by the end of February. And third, while I'm all for monthly business reviews, you can't wait an entire month to check in on your numbers. As we've already discussed...
the session on company scorecards, metrics need to be reviewed weekly, and most strategic planning frameworks stop at monthly. The scalable planning framework is a a proven three-part strategic planning and meeting cadence where companies, one, plan in three-year cycles instead of ludicrously long five-and ten-year cycles. Two, execute in 90-day sprints instead of inaccurate annual plans. And three, measure in weekly scorecard meetings. Instead of waiting a full month to know what the heck is going on. So here's how it works. First, a leadership team gathers at a one-day strategic planning offsite. We call a clarity day. It is during this clarity day where the three-year target is set along with the company's purpose, core values, and strategic anchors. Once a clear three-year target is set, it can then be broken down into 12 90-day sprints. These 12, 90-day sprints are further broken down into 12 one-week measurement intervals, which is where we get the mantra to plan in three-year cycles. Execute in 90-day sprints, and measure using weekly scorecards. So now that you understand the structure of the scalable planning system, let's break down each part and see how they fit into your broader company meeting rhythm.
